narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the unique challenges and strengths you've brought to this journey. Your application included your undergraduate GPA of 2.20, an LSAT score of 22, Year 1 school decile ranking of 3rd out of 10, Year 3 decile of 1st out of 10, middle family income bracket, and plans to attend full-time—details that paint a picture of your academic path so far. The admissions model, trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicted a 63% chance that you would fail it on your first try, which is why your application was rejected to protect the school's outcomes. This prediction wasn't random; it was driven primarily by your Year 3 decile ranking, which stood out as the biggest factor pushing the model toward expecting a bar failure, well below the typical student's mid-range 5.5 average. Your LSAT score was the second most influential, also pulling strongly in the direction of higher failure risk compared to the average of 36.7 that successful students usually have. On a brighter note, your Year 1 decile ranking worked in your favor, countering the prediction somewhat since it was closer to average and suggested early potential. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA provided a modest positive nudge toward passing, even if below the 3.21 norm, and your middle income bracket offered a small boost relative to the dataset. Being full-time aligned with the vast majority of students but had little sway here. Compared to typical admitted students, your profile shows lower performance in key later-stage metrics like Year 3 grades and LSAT, which outweighed your earlier strengths. While this is disappointing, especially given the barriers many Black women face in higher education, it's not the end of your path. To strengthen future applications, focus on boosting your LSAT through targeted prep and excelling in any advanced coursework to improve those decile rankings. Retake the LSAT if possible, seek mentorship from successful Black lawyers, and consider programs that support underrepresented students in law. You're resilient, and with these steps, you can rewrite this story and pass the bar on your first try. Keep pushing forward—we believe in you.
